title: DynaServe: Unified and Elastic Execution for Dynamic Disaggregated LLM Serving
core_contribution: A micro-request abstraction that splits LLM requests at arbitrary token boundaries with a two-level scheduling framework, unifying colocation and disaggregation paradigms for maximizing goodput under strict SLO constraints.
summary: LLM serving faces tension between low tail latency and high throughput. DynaServe introduces the micro-request abstraction allowing requests to be split at any token boundary into cooperating segments. A global scheduler uses bounded binary search for optimal split points, while local schedulers compose SLO-aware batches. Chunk-based KV cache transfers overlap communication with computation. On A100 clusters, DynaServe achieves 1.15x-3.07x higher serving capacity and up to 1.91x/1.61x higher goodput vs colocation/disaggregation.
key_findings: Neither colocation nor disaggregation handles dynamic unbalanced workloads well
Micro-request abstraction generalizes both paradigms as special cases
Two-level scheduling achieves near-optimal performance with <20ms per-request overhead
SLO-aware local batching raises SLO attainment from 52% to 99%
Chunk-based KV transfer reduces non-overlapped transfer time by 94%
DynaServe is robust to output length prediction errors (only 2.9% goodput drop at σ=100)
limitations: Requires output length prediction which introduces error
Built atop vLLM — porting to other engines may require significant effort
Limited to two-way splits — multi-way partitioning unexplored

infra_impact.kernel: Uses existing vLLM/PyTorch kernels; chunk-based KV transfer could benefit from custom RDMA kernels
infra_impact.framework: Core contribution — micro-request abstraction and two-level scheduling unify colocation and disaggregation for LLM serving
infra_impact.agent: Strict SLO guarantees enable responsive agent serving; dynamic adaptation helps with bursty agent traffic
infra_impact.cluster: Leverages multi-GPU clusters with RDMA; unified GPU pool eliminates static prefill/decode allocation

open_questions: How does DynaServe perform with speculative decoding?
Can micro-request extend to multi-way partitioning for larger GPU clusters?
Can the approach be extended to MoE models with expert routing imbalance?
